body_txt: Many "hard" Science Fiction authors, trained as they are in the hard sciences, such as chemistry or physics, tend to take digs at the "softer" sciences (social sciences such as sociology and anthropology) in their works. The reasons for this vary, but the most common criticism is that it's much harder to perform repeatable experiments. Scientists strive towards empiricism and the "scientific method," many humanities or social sciences are trying to study things that cannot easily be studied strictly and subjected to experimentation, making hard science writers feel justified in considering them as "pseudoscience." This happens outside of science fiction as well. Often, scientist characters in non-sci-fi shows will insult the softer sciences when they have to deal with them. In real life, common targets include Psychology, Psychiatry (often portrayed as the medical equivalent of the Church of Happyology , which is ironic considering the church's attitude towards it), Economics ("The Dismal Science") and Linguistics. Some can have a grudging respect for economics and political science, which can tell if one will get more funding soon. Think of it as an interdisciplinary Take That! . How much the rivalry is Serious Business , and how much friendly banter, depends on the characters involved. You can expect scientists to focus on the rationalism and empirical foundation of their work, while characters in the humanities and social sciences will argue that their work is more applicable and relevant to life and society and large — a world with science but without art or literature is a cold, dull world, that sort of thing. Usually the aesop in these stories is that both perspectives and worldviews are vital to a full understanding of the world, but that will, of course, depend on the writer's own background and preferences. Compare and contrast Science Is Bad and Science Is Wrong . See M.D. Envy and Not That Kind of Doctor , which can be related. There may also be a bit of overlap with A Degree in Useless , as students of the hard sciences may consider degrees in soft sciences to count as one. Not related to erections in any way and, more specifically, not related to the Sci-Fi Ghetto .
